tipRules.tip: KVD: In clear water, they'll bite the Ned Rig on the fall. Cast out, let it sink on dead slack line — don't tighten up until it hits bottom.
KVD: In heavy wind and deep water, bump up to 3/16 oz Ned head. In shallower water, use 1/16 oz — lighter the head, the better the action.

newOpinions.defaultTip: KVD: Cast, let it sink on dead slack line, then just drag real slow keeping the jig head on the bottom. The mushroom head makes it stand up — that's what's special. If you miss one, immediately drop it right back — they'll come back and bite it.

knowledge.insight: KVD: 7'2" medium light with extra fast tip (GS4 LSE KVD rod). Uses braid for maximum feel — 'with that braid, you can feel everything.' Bites can be very subtle.

KVD: Don't set the hook traditionally on a Ned Rig. Reel into them fast — let the rod load, then pull. If you miss one, immediately drop it right back down because they'll come right back and bite it.

KVD: The Ned Rig is so popular for smallmouth because it imitates gobies. Gobies stay locked on the bottom on rocks — that's exactly where the Ned Rig sits. Find the rocks, you find the big smallmouth. Gobies have changed these fisheries — fish don't have to move because the food source is so prolific.
KVD: Use the lightest Ned head you can get away with. 1/16 oz in shallow, 3/16 oz in deep water or heavy wind. Lighter head = better action on the bait.

KVD: When fishing the Ned Rig on an edge, the main thing is keeping yourself positioned on the edge where the fish are. Spot Lock (trolling motor GPS anchor) is essential — 'without that spot lock, I would never be able to stay positioned or hold or land one.'
